questions.stem: A 2-week-old full-term infant is found to have a harsh pan-systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border during a routine check. The infant is feeding well and has no cyanosis or respiratory distress. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A harsh pan-systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border is classic for a VSD. ASD has a fixed split S2 and ejection systolic murmur; PDA has continuous machinery murmur; TOF would have cyanosis; mild PS has ejection systolic murmur at left upper sternal border.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child presents with mild exertional dyspnea. On examination, there is a fixed, widely split second heart sound and an ejection systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border. ECG shows right axis deviation and incomplete right bundle branch block. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fixed wide splitting of S2 is pathognomonic for ASD. VSD and PDA do not cause fixed split; PAPVR can mimic ASD but is less common; PS has ejection click and normal splitting.

questions.stem: An 8-month-old infant with known Tetralogy of Fallot presents with sudden irritability, deepening cyanosis, and decreased intensity of the systolic murmur. The parents report a similar episode last week that resolved after the infant was held in a knee-chest position. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic presentation of a Tetralogy spell: cyanosis, irritability, decreased murmur due to reduced pulmonary blood flow. Knee-chest position increases systemic resistance, helping to differentiate. Other options do not typically show this pattern.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a known small ventricular septal defect presents with progressive dyspnea, cyanosis, and clubbing. She has had hemoptysis twice in the past month. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This is a classic late complication of a left-to-right shunt (VSD) with reversal of shunt leading to cyanosis, clubbing, and hemoptysis. Infective endocarditis would present with fever and signs of infection; PE would be acute; IPAH is not associated with previous VSD; COPD is unlikely in a young woman.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old university student is noted to have hypertension (160/95 mmHg) during a routine health check. He has a history of frequent headaches but no other symptoms. On examination, radial pulses are bounding but femoral pulses are weak and delayed. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic sign of coarctation is hypertension in the arms with weak, delayed femoral pulses. Essential hypertension does not have pulse discrepancy; renal artery stenosis may cause hypertension but pulses are normal; aortic stenosis presents with a murmur and decreased pulses generally; PDA causes continuous murmur and bounding pulses, but not differential.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old term neonate is noted to be cyanotic. On examination, there is a systolic ejection murmur at the left upper sternal border. Oxygen saturation is 75% in room air. Chest X-ray shows a 'boot-shaped' heart. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The boot-shaped heart and cyanosis suggest Tetralogy of Fallot. Echocardiography is the definitive first-line investigation to confirm the anatomy and assess severity, whereas cardiac catheterisation is reserved for complex cases or pre-intervention planning.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl is noted to have a continuous 'machinery' murmur at the left infraclavicular area. She is asymptomatic. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The machinery murmur is classic for patent ductus arteriosus. Echocardiography with colour Doppler demonstrates the shunt and is the gold standard for diagnosis; ECG and CXR may be normal or non-specific.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant presents with tachypnoea and poor weight gain. On examination, there is a pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and bounding pulses. Chest X-ray is performed. Which of the following chest X-ray findings is most likely?
options: Cardiomegaly with reduced pulmonary vascular markings
Cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings
Normal heart size and lung fields
questions.explanation: The clinical picture suggests a large left-to-right shunt (likely VSD). This leads to increased pulmonary blood flow, causing cardiomegaly and increased pulmonary vascular markings. Reduced markings favour right-to-left shunt; 'egg-on-side' is typical of TAPVR; rib notching occurs in coarctation in older children.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child is brought for evaluation of a murmur. Electrocardiogram shows a superior QRS axis (negative in aVF). Which congenital heart defect is most likely?
questions.explanation: A superior QRS axis is a classic ECG finding in atrioventricular septal defects, due to abnormal ventricular conduction. The other options typically show different axis deviations: ASD often has incomplete RBBB, VSD normal axis, PDA normal axis, Tetralogy right axis deviation.

questions.stem: A neonate presents with weak femoral pulses and differential cyanosis (lower body cyanosis). Pre-ductal oxygen saturation is 98%, post-ductal saturation is 70%. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The clinical picture is classic for critical coarctation of the aorta with duct-dependent systemic circulation. Echocardiography is the first-line investigation to visualise the coarctation and ductus; CT aortogram is not first-line due to radiation and contrast.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old neonate presents with severe shock and a weak femoral pulse. Echocardiography confirms critical aortic stenosis with duct-dependent systemic circulation. The baby is being prepared for urgent surgical intervention. Which is the most appropriate first-line medical therapy to maintain ductal patency?
questions.explanation: In duct-dependent lesions such as critical aortic stenosis, a continuous prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) infusion is the first-line treatment to maintain patency of the ductus arteriosus, ensuring systemic perfusion until definitive surgery. Ibuprofen is used for PDA closure, not to keep it open. Furosemide and digoxin are supportive but do not maintain ductal patency.

questions.stem: A preterm infant born at 28 weeks gestation has a haemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) with bounding pulses and wide pulse pressure. What is the first-line pharmacological treatment for closure of the PDA?
questions.explanation: Intravenous ibuprofen is the first-line pharmacological treatment for symptomatic PDA in preterm infants in the UK, as it is effective with fewer renal side effects than indomethacin. Paracetamol is a second-line option. Surgical ligation and transcatheter closure are reserved for contraindications or failure of medical therapy.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with known tetralogy of Fallot develops a hypercyanotic spell after crying. The infant is cyanosed and tachypnoeic. Which is the most appropriate first-line drug therapy for this acute episode?
questions.explanation: In a hypercyanotic spell, immediate first-line treatment is morphine to reduce infundibular spasm and anxiety. Propranolol is used for prophylaxis or if spells recur. Prostaglandin is not indicated, phenylephrine may increase afterload but is not first-line, and sodium bicarbonate is for acidosis only after other measures.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old child with a large unrepaired ventricular septal defect has developed Eisenmenger syndrome with irreversible pulmonary hypertension and a right-to-left shunt. Which of the following treatments is CONTRAINDICATED in this patient?
questions.explanation: In Eisenmenger syndrome, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers like nifedipine are contraindicated because they can cause systemic hypotension, increasing the right-to-left shunt and worsening cyanosis. Targeted pulmonary vasodilators (sildenafil, treprostinil, iloprost, epoprostenol) are beneficial and improve symptoms.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old child with repaired coarctation of the aorta has persistent resting hypertension (blood pressure >95th centile for age) despite lifestyle advice. Which antihypertensive agent is most appropriate as first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: In post-coarctation hypertension, an ACE inhibitor such as ramipril is often first-line due to activation of the renin-angiotensin system from prior renal hypoperfusion. Beta-blockers are also used but ACE inhibitors are preferred in NICE guidelines for this context. Amlodipine, thiazides, and alpha-blockers are second- or third-line.

questions.stem: A 2-day-old term neonate presents with progressive cyanosis and tachypnoea. Oxygen saturations are 70% in the right arm and 65% in the lower limb. Chest X-ray shows a normal-sized heart with decreased pulmonary vascular markings. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange urgent transfer to paediatric cardiology centre
questions.explanation: In a cyanotic neonate with signs of duct-dependent systemic circulation (e.g., hypoplastic left heart syndrome), maintaining ductal patency with prostaglandin E1 is the priority before any investigations or transfer. The hyperoxia test and oxygen may worsen the situation if the lesion is duct-dependent.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child attends cardiology clinic for routine follow-up of a small perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD). The child is asymptomatic, growing well, and has no murmur. Echocardiogram from 2 years ago showed a restrictive VSD with left-to-right shunt and no pulmonary hypertension. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Discharge from follow-up with GP advice
questions.explanation: A small, restrictive VSD that is asymptomatic with no pulmonary hypertension and no significant shunt can be discharged from follow-up. Repeated imaging or intervention is not indicated unless complications arise.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant with known Tetralogy of Fallot is brought to the emergency department crying and cyanosed. The nurse reports the infant has had several brief episodes of deep cyanosis with crying. On examination, the infant is irritable, oxygen saturation 60%, and there is a harsh systolic murmur. What is the single most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Place the infant in a knee-chest position
questions.explanation: In a tet spell, the first action is to increase systemic vascular resistance by placing the infant in a knee-chest position to reduce right-to-left shunting. Oxygen and morphine may be given subsequently, but the knee-chest position is the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy with repaired Tetralogy of Fallot (correction at age 2) presents with increasing exercise intolerance and palpitations. Echocardiogram shows severe pulmonary regurgitation with right ventricular dilation. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
questions.explanation: Cardiac MRI is the gold standard for assessing right ventricular volumes and function before deciding on pulmonary valve replacement. Although valve replacement is likely, MRI provides precise quantification and timing.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old woman with a history of repaired coarctation of the aorta at age 5 is noted to have a blood pressure of 155/95 mmHg in her right arm and 130/80 in her left arm. She is asymptomatic. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Order a CT angiogram of the aorta
Perform echocardiography to assess for recoarctation
questions.explanation: Hypertension with a differential between arms raises suspicion for recoarctation. Echocardiography is the first-line imaging to assess the aortic arch and exclude significant narrowing before initiating medication or further imaging.

questions.stem: A 4-day-old female presents with cyanosis and tachypnoea. Oxygen saturations are 92% in the right hand and 75% in the left foot. Which clinical finding most strongly suggests a duct-dependent cardiac lesion?
questions.explanation: Differential cyanosis with lower saturations in the lower limbs indicates deoxygenated blood entering the descending aorta via a patent ductus arteriosus, a hallmark of duct-dependent systemic circulation (e.g., critical coarctation or interrupted aortic arch). Central cyanosis, tachypnoea, murmur, and hepatomegaly are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old infant with a large perimembranous ventricular septal defect (VSD) is seen in follow-up. She has poor weight gain despite high-calorie feeds. On auscultation, the murmur is still pansystolic. Which clinical finding would raise concern for developing pulmonary hypertension?
questions.explanation: A loud P2 suggests elevated pulmonary artery pressure, which can progress to Eisenmenger syndrome if untreated. Wide fixed splitting is typical of atrial septal defect. A mid-diastolic murmur is often from increased flow across the mitral valve. Loud S1 and gallop are non-specific.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child with known tetralogy of Fallot presents with a cyanotic spell: irritable, deep breathing, oxygen saturation 60% on room air. Which clinical finding best distinguishes a hypercyanotic spell from a respiratory infection?
options: Harsh systolic murmur at the left sternal edge
questions.explanation: During a hypercyanotic spell, infundibular spasm reduces pulmonary blood flow, causing the murmur to soften or disappear. This is a key differentiating feature. Clubbing and a harsh murmur are chronic findings. Hepatomegaly and differential cyanosis are not specific.

questions.stem: A 14-year-old female with repaired tetralogy of Fallot presents with palpitations. She is haemodynamically stable. ECG shows a regular wide QRS tachycardia. Which ECG finding most indicates ventricular tachycardia rather than supraventricular tachycardia with aberrancy?
options: P waves visible in lead II
questions.explanation: AV dissociation (independent atrial and ventricular activity) is diagnostic of ventricular tachycardia. While QRS duration and morphology can be clues, they are not definitive. P waves visible in lead II are normal. Repaired TOF patients are at risk for ventricular arrhythmias.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old male with repaired coarctation of the aorta presents with severe headache and blurred vision. Blood pressure is 210/120 mmHg in the right arm. Which physical finding would indicate a hypertensive emergency?
questions.explanation: Papilloedema or retinal haemorrhages on fundoscopy confirm hypertensive emergency requiring immediate treatment. Radiofemoral delay is a chronic sign of coarctation. Bounding pulses, S3 gallop, and diastolic murmur are not specific for acute emergency.
